<hr style="height:0pt; visibility:hidden;" />

# pycodestyle

Checks against PEP-8 style guidelines.

```{python}
#| echo: false
# Import required packages
import subprocess
import sys

# Add parent directory to sys.path
sys.path.insert(0, "..")

# Import the helper functions
from helpers import generate_html, print_quarto
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| output: asis
generate_html(
    pypi_url="https://pypi.org/project/pycodestyle/",
    github_url="https://github.com/PyCQA/pycodestyle",
    conda_url="https://anaconda.org/conda-forge/pycodestyle"
)
```

## Example Quarto `.qmd` file:

{{< downloadthis examples/general_example.qmd dname="general_example" label="Download the example" type="light" >}}

:::{.pale-grey}
```{python}
#| echo: false
print_quarto("examples/general_example.qmd")
```
:::

## Run pycodestyle using `lintquarto`

```{.bash}
lintquarto -l pycodestyle -p general_example.qmd
```

:::{.pale-green}
<style>
.cell-output-stdout code {
  word-break: break-word !important;
  white-space: pre-wrap !important;
}
</style>
```{python}
#| echo: false
result = subprocess.run(
    [
        sys.executable,
        "-m",
        "lintquarto",
        "-l",
        "pycodestyle",
        "-p",
        "examples/general_example.qmd"
    ],
    capture_output=True,
    text=True,
    check=False
)
print(result.stdout)
```
:::

## Known limitation for pycodestyle with lintquarto

`lintquarto` works by converting `.qmd` files into temporary `.py` files. During this process, non-code regions (e.g., markdown and cell boundaries) are replaced with placeholder lines (`# -`) so that line numbers remain aligned between the original `.qmd` file and the generated `.py` file. Linters are then run on the generated Python file.

This approach causes issues with `pycodestyle` for the E3xx "blank line/layout" rules (e.g., E302, E305). The inserted placeholder lines can cause `pycodestyle` to report false positive violations of these rules - as can be seen in the example above. While these false positives can be suppressed by `lintquarto` for other packages using `# noqa`,`pycodestyle` only supports `# noqa` for a limited subset of error codes and does not allow suppression of E3xx rules in this way. As a result, these false positives cannot be reliably ignored when using `pycodestyle` directly. This is a known limitation ([#142](https://github.com/lintquarto/lintquarto/issues/142)).

### Work around

If you want to apply `pycodestyle` checks to Quarto files without these false positives, we recommend using a wrapper tool like **flake8** or **ruff**. These tools include `pycodestyle` rules but allow suppression of E3xx rules via `# noqa`, making it possible to ignore the conversion-related violations.

[**Flake8:**](flake8.qmd) You can restrict checks to `pycodestyle` rules via `setup.cfg`:

```
# setup.cfg
[flake8]
select = E,W
```

[**Ruff:**](ruff.qmd) You can restrict checks to `pycodestyle` rules via `pyproject.toml`:

```
# pyproject.toml
[tool.ruff.lint]
extend-select = ["E", "W"]
```
